name: Little Drum Mountains
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in central Utah known for agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. Easy access with no permit required for personal collecting.
description:
The Little Drum Mountains in Millard County offer accessible rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land with minimal regulatory barriers. This remote desert area sits within Utah's Basin and Range province, where extensive silica mineralization has created favorable conditions for collecting quality agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens.

The terrain here features gently rolling sagebrush-covered hills interspersed with rocky outcrops and washes where weathered stones accumulate. Collectors typically find specimens ranging from thumbnail size to fist-sized pieces, with better quality material concentrated in disturbed areas along old wash channels and erosional features. The volcanic and sedimentary geological framework of this region has produced varied colors including translucent whites, honey tones, and patterned specimens.

Access is straightforward for experienced rockhounds seeking a quiet, uncrowded site. The BLM designation means no permits are required and collecting follows standard personal-use guidelines. Water and services are limited, so plan accordingly for a full day of collecting. This location rewards patient, systematic searching rather than quick surface grabs.

Best results come from working wash beds, inspecting exposed ridgelines, and following natural drainage patterns where heavier specimens accumulate. The site sees minimal foot traffic, so material availability remains relatively consistent season to season.
history: The Little Drum Mountains lie within the Basin and Range physiographic province, shaped by Cenozoic extension and volcanism. Miocene-age volcanic activity deposited silica-rich rhyolitic and andesitic rocks that have weathered over millions of years to expose chalcedony nodules, agate seams, and jasper beds throughout this region.
tips: Focus your search in the washes and canyon bottoms where erosion concentrates heavier stones - this is where the best agate and chalcedony specimens accumulate naturally. Bring a hand lens to inspect specimens for color and translucency before packing them out; many pieces that look dull on the surface polish beautifully once cleaned. Work the base of rocky outcrops and exposed ledges where material has weathered out of the host rock. Early morning or late afternoon light improves your ability to spot translucent specimens against the surrounding rock.
terrain_notes: Rolling sagebrush-covered hills with scattered rocky outcrops and seasonal wash channels. Elevation approximately 5,000-5,500 feet. Terrain is uneven but navigable for most hikers, with loose rock, scattered vegetation, and minimal maintained trails. Distances from parking to productive collecting areas typically 0.5 to 2 miles. Footing requires sturdy footwear due to sharp volcanic fragments and unstable slopes.
best_season: April - June, September - November. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures for hiking and collecting. Summer heat (100+ degrees F) makes midday collecting uncomfortable; winter brings occasional snow at this elevation.
written_directions: From Delta, Utah (approximately 35 miles southwest), drive east on US 50 for about 25 miles toward Salina. Turn north on a county road toward the Little Drum Mountains area. Exact access roads require current BLM maps or local knowledge; contact the BLM Salina Field Office for specific pullout locations and road status, as seasonal conditions affect accessibility. GPS coordinates 39.462473, -113.030296 mark the general rockhounding area.
road_conditions: BLM dirt roads, potentially washboarded or rutted. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle recommended, especially after rain. Road conditions vary seasonally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM roads near collecting areas. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space for 2-3 vehicles at typical spots.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or powered equipment. Collect only loose material and small nodules; do not hammer or chisel bedrock. Pack out all waste. Stay on or near existing roads and trails to minimize surface disturbance.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or powered tools
No hammering or chiseling bedrock
Pack out all waste
Stay on or near existing roads and trails
nearest_services: Delta, Utah (approximately 35 miles southwest) has gas, food, and lodging. Salina, Utah (approximately 25 miles east) offers limited services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven, rocky footing and scattered sagebrush. Loose rock and sharp volcanic fragments present underfoot. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Trail-less collecting requires navigating open desert terrain without marked paths. Most productive areas require 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking from vehicle.

faq.question: What types of agates and stones can I find rockhounding at Little Drum Mountains?
faq.answer: Little Drum Mountains is known for quality agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. Colors typically include whites, honey tones, translucent varieties, and patterned material. Specimens range from thumbnail to fist-sized pieces. The best translucent agates are found in wash beds where erosion has concentrated heavier stones.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Little Drum Mountains in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal rockhounding on this BLM public land. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day under FLPMA personal-use regulations. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Delta, Utah?
faq.answer: April through June and September through November are ideal. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and stable weather. Summer temperatures exceed 100 degrees F and make daytime collecting difficult. Winter can bring snow at this 5,000+ foot elevation.

faq.question: How far is Little Drum Mountains from Delta, Utah and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: Little Drum Mountains is approximately 35 miles from Delta. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended since access requires BLM dirt roads that can be washboarded or rutted, especially after rain. Road conditions vary seasonally.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Little Drum Mountains good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is beginner and family-friendly. No special skills are required, and collecting loose material is straightforward. Children ages 8 and up can participate with adult supervision. The moderate difficulty involves uneven terrain and hiking 0.5-2 miles to productive areas.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for collecting agates at Little Drum Mountains?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a backpack, a 5-gallon bucket or bag for specimens, a hand lens to inspect material, and sun protection. Avoid hammers and chisels since motorized excavation and bedrock hammering are not permitted. Bring plenty of water as the desert is remote with no services nearby.

meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and jasper at Little Drum Mountains on BLM land. No permit required for personal collecting. Easy access near Delta, Utah.
